(**FRG**)[[b]](#cite_note-6) from its formation on 23 May 1949 until [its reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification") with [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") on [3 October 1990](/wiki/German_Unity_Day "German Unity Day"). It was sometimes known as the **Bonn Republic**[[c]](#cite_note-7) after its capital city of [Bonn](/wiki/Bonn "Bonn"), or as the **Second German Republic**.[[5]](#cite_note-8) During the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), the western portion of Germany and the associated territory of [West Berlin](/wiki/West_Berlin "West Berlin") were parts of the [Western Bloc](/wiki/Western_Bloc "Western Bloc"). West Germany was formed as a political entity during the [Allied occupation of Germany](/wiki/Allied_occupation_of_Germany "Allied occupation of Germany") after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), established from twelve [states](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") formed in the three Allied zones of occupation held by the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), and [France](/wiki/French_Fourth_Republic "French Fourth Republic").

**West Germany**[[a]](#cite_note-5) was the common English name for the **Federal Republic of Germany** (**FRG**)[[b]](#cite_note-6) from its formation on 23 May 1949 until [its reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification") with [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") on [3 October 1990](/wiki/German_Unity_Day "German Unity Day"). It was sometimes known as the **Bonn Republic**[[c]](#cite_note-7) after its capital city of [Bonn](/wiki/Bonn "Bonn"), or as the **Second German Republic**.[[5]](#cite_note-8) During the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), the western portion of Germany and the associated territory of [West Berlin](/wiki/West_Berlin "West Berlin") were parts of the [Western Bloc](/wiki/Western_Bloc "Western Bloc"). West Germany was formed as a political entity during the [Allied occupation of Germany](/wiki/Allied_occupation_of_Germany "Allied occupation of Germany") after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), established from twelve [states](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") formed in the three Allied zones of occupation held by the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), and [France](/wiki/French_Fourth_Republic "French Fourth Republic").

At the onset of the Cold War, [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") was divided between the Western and [Eastern blocs](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc"). Germany was divided into the two countries. Initially, West Germany claimed an [exclusive mandate](/wiki/Exclusive_mandate "Exclusive mandate") for all of Germany, representing itself as the sole democratically reorganised continuation of the 1871‚Äď1945 [German Reich](/wiki/German_Reich "German Reich").[[6]](#cite_note-9)

Three southwestern states of West Germany merged to form [Baden-W√ľrttemberg](/wiki/Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Baden-W√ľrttemberg") in 1952, and the [Saarland](/wiki/Saarland "Saarland") joined West Germany as a state in 1957 after it had been separated as the [Saar Protectorate](/wiki/Saar_Protectorate "Saar Protectorate") from [Allied-occupied Germany](/wiki/Allied-occupied_Germany "Allied-occupied Germany") by France (the separation had not been legal as it had not been recognised by the [Allied Control Council](/wiki/Allied_Control_Council "Allied Control Council")). In addition to the resulting ten states, West Berlin was considered an unofficial *de facto* eleventh state. While *de jure* not part of West Germany, for Berlin was under the control of the Allied Control Council, West Berlin politically aligned itself with West Germany and was directly or indirectly represented in its federal institutions.

The foundation for the influential position held by Germany today was laid during the economic miracle of the 1950s (*[Wirtschaftswunder](/wiki/Wirtschaftswunder "Wirtschaftswunder")*), when West Germany rose from the enormous destruction wrought by World War II to become the world's second-largest economy. The first [Chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany "Chancellor of Germany") [Konrad Adenauer](/wiki/Konrad_Adenauer "Konrad Adenauer"), who remained in office until 1963, worked for a full alignment with the [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") rather than neutrality, and secured membership in the military alliance. Adenauer was also a proponent of agreements that developed into the present-day [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"). When the [G6](/wiki/G7 "G7") was established in 1975, there was no serious debate as to whether West Germany would become a member.

Following the [collapse of the Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1989 "Revolutions of 1989"), symbolised by the [opening](/wiki/Fall_of_the_Berlin_Wall "Fall of the Berlin Wall") of the [Berlin Wall](/wiki/Berlin_Wall "Berlin Wall"), both states took action to achieve [German reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification"). East Germany voted to dissolve and accede to the Federal Republic of Germany in 1990. The [five post-war states](/wiki/New_states_of_Germany "New states of Germany") (*L√§nder*) were reconstituted, along with the reunited [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), which ended its special status and formed a sixth *Land*. They formally joined the federal republic on 3 October 1990, raising the total number of states from ten to 16, and ending the division of Germany. The reunited Germany is thus the direct continuation of the state previously informally called West Germany and not a new state, as the process was essentially a voluntary act of accession: the Federal Republic of Germany was enlarged to include the additional six states of the dissolved German Democratic Republic. The expanded Federal Republic retained West Germany's political culture and continued its existing memberships in international organisations, as well as its Western foreign policy alignment and affiliation to Western alliances such as the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), NATO, [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD"), and the [European Economic Community](/wiki/European_Economic_Community "European Economic Community").

## Naming conventions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Germany&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Naming conventions")]

Before reunification, Germany was divided between the *Bundesrepublik Deutschland* (Federal Republic of Germany; commonly known as West Germany) and the *Deutsche Demokratische Republik* (DDR; German Democratic Republic; commonly known as East Germany). Reunification was achieved by accession (*Beitritt*) of the German Democratic Republic to the Federal Republic of Germany, so *Bundesrepublik Deutschland* became the official name of reunified Germany.

In East Germany, the terms *Westdeutschland* (West Germany) or *westdeutsche Bundesrepublik* (West German Federal Republic) were preferred during the 1950s and 1960s. This changed under its constitutional amendment in 1974, when the idea of a single German nation was abandoned by East Germany. As a result, it officially considered West Germans and West Berliners as foreigners. The initialism *[BRD](/wiki/BRD_(Germany) "BRD (Germany)")* (FRG in English) began to prevail in East German usage in the early 1970s, beginning in the newspaper *[Neues Deutschland](/wiki/Neues_Deutschland "Neues Deutschland")*. Other [Eastern Bloc](/wiki/Eastern_Bloc "Eastern Bloc") nations soon followed suit.

In 1965, the West German [Federal Minister of All-German Affairs](/wiki/Minister_of_Intra-German_Relations "Minister of Intra-German Relations"), [Erich Mende](/wiki/Erich_Mende "Erich Mende"), had issued the "Directives for the Appellation of Germany", recommending avoiding the initialism BRD. On 31 May 1974, the heads of West German federal and state governments recommended always using the full name in official publications. From then on, West German sources avoided the abbreviated form, with the exception of left-leaning organisations which embraced it. In November 1979, the federal government informed the [Bundestag](/wiki/Bundestag "Bundestag") that the West German public broadcasters [ARD](/wiki/ARD_(broadcaster) "ARD (broadcaster)") and [ZDF](/wiki/ZDF "ZDF") had agreed to refuse to use the initialism.[[7]](#cite_note-10)

The [ISO 3166-1 alpha-2](/wiki/ISO_3166-1_alpha-2 "ISO 3166-1 alpha-2") country code of West Germany was DE (for *Deutschland*, Germany), which has remained the country code of Germany after reunification. ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 codes are the most widely used country codes, and the DE code is notably used as a country identifier, extending the postal code and as the Internet's country code top-level domain [.de](/wiki/.de ".de"). The less widely used [ISO 3166-1 alpha-3](/wiki/ISO_3166-1_alpha-3 "ISO 3166-1 alpha-3") country code of West Germany was DEU, which has remained the country code of reunified Germany. The now deleted codes for East Germany, on the other hand, were DD in ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 and DDR in ISO 3166-1 alpha-3.

The colloquial term *West Germany* or its equivalent was used in many languages. *Westdeutschland* was also a widespread colloquial form used in German-speaking countries, usually without political overtones.

The territories east of the [Oder‚ÄďNeisse line](/wiki/Oder%E2%80%93Neisse_line "Oder‚ÄďNeisse line"), under Polish and Soviet administration/annexation, are shown in cream, as is the detached [Saar Protectorate](/wiki/Saar_Protectorate "Saar Protectorate"). [Bremen](/wiki/Bremen_(state) "Bremen (state)") was an American [enclave](/wiki/Enclave_and_exclave "Enclave and exclave") within the British zone. Berlin was a four-power area within the Soviet zone.

On 4‚Äď11 February 1945 leaders from the United States, the United Kingdom, and the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union") held the [Yalta Conference](/wiki/Yalta_Conference "Yalta Conference") where future arrangements regarding post-war Europe and [Allied strategy against Japan in the Pacific](/wiki/Pacific_War "Pacific War") were negotiated. They agreed that the boundaries of Germany as at 31 December 1937 would be chosen as demarcating German national territory from German-occupied territory; all German annexations after 1937 were automatically null. Subsequently, and into the 1970s, the West German state was to maintain that these 1937 boundaries continued to be 'valid in international law', although the Allies had already agreed amongst themselves that the territories east of the [Oder‚ÄďNeisse line](/wiki/Oder%E2%80%93Neisse_line "Oder‚ÄďNeisse line") must be transferred to Poland and the Soviet Union in any peace agreement. The conference agreed that post-war Germany, minus these transfers, would be divided into [four occupation zones](/wiki/Allied-occupied_Germany "Allied-occupied Germany"): a French Zone in the far west; a British Zone in the northwest; an American Zone in the south; and a Soviet Zone in the East. Berlin was separately divided into four zones. These divisions were not intended to dismember Germany, only to designate zones of administration.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Vogelsang_%28West-Duitsland%29._Legeroefeningen_32e_regiment._Centuriontanks_en_infa%2C_Bestanddeelnr_050-0522.jpg/250px-Vogelsang_%28West-Duitsland%29._Legeroefeningen_32e_regiment._Centuriontanks_en_infa%2C_Bestanddeelnr_050-0522.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vogelsang_(West-Duitsland)._Legeroefeningen_32e_regiment._Centuriontanks_en_infa,_Bestanddeelnr_050-0522.jpg)

Dutch tanks, pictured in West Germany in 1956 as part of the large British and American-led foreign military presence in the country

By the subsequent [Potsdam Agreement](/wiki/Potsdam_Agreement "Potsdam Agreement"), the four Allied Powers asserted joint sovereignty over "Germany as a whole", defined as the totality of the territory within the occupation zones. Former German areas east of the rivers [Oder](/wiki/Oder "Oder") and [Neisse](/wiki/Lusatian_Neisse "Lusatian Neisse") and outside of 'Germany as a whole' were officially separated from German sovereignty in August 1945 and transferred from Soviet military occupation to Polish and Soviet (in the case of the territory of Kaliningrad) civil administration, their Polish and Soviet status to be confirmed at a final Peace Treaty. Following wartime commitments by the Allies to the governments-in-exile of Czechoslovakia and Poland, the Potsdam Protocols also agreed to the 'orderly and humane' transfer to Germany as a whole of the ethnic German populations in Poland, Czechoslovakia and Hungary. Eight million German expellees and refugees eventually settled in West Germany. Between 1946 and 1949, three of the occupation zones began to merge. First, the British and American zones were combined into the quasi-state of [Bizonia](/wiki/Bizone "Bizone"). Soon afterwards, the French zone was included into [Trizonia](/wiki/Bizone "Bizone"). Conversely, the Soviet zone became [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany"). At the same time, new federal states (*L√§nder*) were formed in the Allied zones; replacing the geography of pre-Nazi German states such as the [Free State of Prussia](/wiki/Free_State_of_Prussia "Free State of Prussia") and the [Republic of Baden](/wiki/Republic_of_Baden "Republic of Baden"), which had derived ultimately from former independent German kingdoms and principalities.

In the dominant post-war narrative of West Germany, the [Nazi](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") regime was characterised as having been a 'criminal' state,[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECollings2015xxiv-11) illegal and illegitimate from the outset; while the [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic") was characterised as having been a 'failed' state,[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECollings2015xv-12) whose inherent institutional and constitutional flaws had been exploited by [Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") in his illegal seizure of dictatorial powers. Consequently, following the death of Hitler in 1945 and the subsequent capitulation of the German Armed Forces, the national political, judicial, administrative, and constitutional instruments of both Nazi Germany and the Weimar Republic were understood as entirely defunct, such that a new West Germany could be established in a condition of constitutional nullity.[[10]](#cite_note-13) Nevertheless, the new West Germany asserted its fundamental continuity with the 'overall' German state that was held to have embodied the unified German people since the [Frankfurt Parliament](/wiki/Frankfurt_Parliament "Frankfurt Parliament") of 1848, and which from 1871 had been represented within the [German Reich](/wiki/German_Reich "German Reich"); albeit that this overall state had become effectively dormant long before 8 May 1945.

In 1949 with the continuation and aggravation of the Cold War (for example, the [Berlin Airlift](/wiki/Berlin_Blockade#Start_of_the_Berlin_Airlift "Berlin Blockade") of 1948‚Äď49), the two German states that had originated in the Western Allied and the Soviet Zones respectively became known internationally as West Germany and East Germany. Commonly known in English as [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany"), the former [Soviet occupation zone in Germany](/wiki/Soviet_occupation_zone_in_Germany "Soviet occupation zone in Germany"), eventually became the *[German Democratic Republic](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany")* or *GDR*. In 1990 West Germany and East Germany jointly signed the [Treaty on the Final Settlement with Respect to Germany](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Final_Settlement_with_Respect_to_Germany "Treaty on the Final Settlement with Respect to Germany") (also known as the "Two-plus-Four Agreement"); by which transitional status of Germany following [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") was definitively ended and the Four Allied powers relinquished their joint residual sovereign authority for Germany as a whole including the area of West Berlin which had officially remained under Allied occupation for the purposes of international and GDR law (a status that the Western countries applied to Berlin as a whole despite the Soviets declaring the end of occupation of East Berlin unilaterally many decades before). The Two-plus-Four Agreement also saw the two parts of Germany confirm their post-war external boundaries as final and irreversible (including the 1945 transfer of former German lands east of the [Oder‚ÄďNeisse line](/wiki/Oder%E2%80%93Neisse_line "Oder‚ÄďNeisse line")), and the Allied Powers confirmed their consent to German Reunification. From 3 October 1990, after the reformation of the GDR's *L√§nder*, the East German states and East Berlin [joined the Federal Republic](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification").

### NATO membership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Germany&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: NATO membership")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Deutschland_Bundeslaender_1957.png/250px-Deutschland_Bundeslaender_1957.png)](/wiki/File:Deutschland_Bundeslaender_1957.png)

West Germany (blue) and West Berlin (yellow) after the accession of the Saarland in 1957 and before the five *L√§nder* from the GDR and East Berlin joined in 1990

With territories and frontiers that coincided largely with the ones of old [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") [East Francia](/wiki/East_Francia "East Francia") and the 19th-century [Napoleonic](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") [Confederation of the Rhine](/wiki/Confederation_of_the_Rhine "Confederation of the Rhine"), the Federal Republic of Germany was founded on 23 May 1949 under the terms of the [Bonn‚ÄďParis conventions](/wiki/Bonn%E2%80%93Paris_conventions "Bonn‚ÄďParis conventions"), whereby it obtained "the full authority of a sovereign state" on 5 May 1955 (although "full sovereignty" was not obtained until the [Two Plus Four Agreement](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Final_Settlement_with_Respect_to_Germany "Treaty on the Final Settlement with Respect to Germany") in 1990).[[d]](#cite_note-Sovereignty-15) The former occupying Western troops remained on the ground, now as part of the [North Atlantic Treaty Organization](/wiki/NATO "NATO") (NATO), which West Germany joined on 9 May 1955, promising to rearm itself soon.[[12]](#cite_note-16)

West Germany became a focus of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") with its juxtaposition to [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany"), a member of the subsequently founded [Warsaw Pact](/wiki/Warsaw_Pact "Warsaw Pact"). The former capital, [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), had been divided into four sectors, with the Western Allies joining their sectors to form [West Berlin](/wiki/West_Berlin "West Berlin"), while the Soviets held [East Berlin](/wiki/East_Berlin "East Berlin"). West Berlin was completely surrounded by East German territory and had suffered a Soviet blockade in 1948‚Äď49, which was overcome by the [Berlin airlift](/wiki/Berlin_Blockade#Start_of_the_Berlin_Airlift "Berlin Blockade").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/86/Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F078072-0004%2C_Konrad_Adenauer.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F078072-0004%2C_Konrad_Adenauer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F078072-0004,_Konrad_Adenauer.jpg)

[Konrad Adenauer](/wiki/Konrad_Adenauer "Konrad Adenauer") was a German statesman who served as the first [chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany "Chancellor of Germany").

The outbreak of the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War") in June 1950 led to US calls to rearm West Germany to help defend Western Europe from the perceived Soviet threat. Germany's partners in the [European Coal and Steel Community](/wiki/European_Coal_and_Steel_Community "European Coal and Steel Community") proposed to establish a [European Defence Community](/wiki/Treaty_establishing_the_European_Defence_Community "Treaty establishing the European Defence Community") (EDC), with an integrated army, navy and air force, composed of the armed forces of its member states. The West German military would be subject to complete EDC control, but the other EDC member states ([Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), France, Italy, [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg") and the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands")) would cooperate in the EDC while maintaining independent control of their own armed forces.

Though the EDC treaty was signed (May 1952), it never entered into force. France's [Gaullists](/wiki/Gaullism "Gaullism") rejected it on the grounds that it threatened national sovereignty, and when the [French National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_(France) "National Assembly (France)") refused to ratify it (August 1954), the treaty died. Then other means had to be found to allow West German rearmament. In response, at the [London and Paris Conferences](/wiki/London_and_Paris_Conferences "London and Paris Conferences"), the [Brussels Treaty](/wiki/Treaty_of_Brussels "Treaty of Brussels") was modified to include West Germany, and to form the [Western European Union](/wiki/Western_European_Union "Western European Union") (WEU). West Germany was to be permitted to rearm (an idea many Germans rejected), and have full sovereign control of its military, called the *[Bundeswehr](/wiki/Bundeswehr "Bundeswehr")*. The WEU, however, would regulate the size of the armed forces permitted to each of its member states. Also, the German constitution prohibited any military action, except in the case of an external attack against Germany or its allies (*B√ľndnisfall*). Also, Germans could reject military service on grounds of conscience, and serve for civil purposes instead.[[13]](#cite_note-17)

The three Western [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") retained occupation powers in Berlin and certain responsibilities for Germany as a whole. Under the new arrangements, the Allies stationed troops within West Germany for NATO defence, pursuant to stationing and status-of-forces agreements. With the exception of 55,000 French troops, Allied forces were under NATO's joint defence command. (France withdrew from the collective military command structure of NATO in 1966.)

### Reforms during the 1960s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Germany&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Reforms during the 1960s")]

[Konrad Adenauer](/wiki/Konrad_Adenauer "Konrad Adenauer") was 73 years old when he became chancellor in 1949, and for this reason he was initially reckoned as a caretaker. However, he ruled for 14 years. The grand statesman of German postwar politics had to be dragged‚ÄĒalmost literally‚ÄĒout of office in 1963.[[14]](#cite_note-18)

Main article: [Spiegel affair](/wiki/Spiegel_affair "Spiegel affair")

In October 1962 the weekly news magazine *[Der Spiegel](/wiki/Der_Spiegel "Der Spiegel")* published an analysis of the West German military defence. The conclusion was that there were several weaknesses in the system. Ten days after publication, the offices of *Der Spiegel* in Hamburg were raided by the police and quantities of documents were seized. Chancellor Adenauer proclaimed in the *Bundestag* that the article was tantamount to high treason and that the authors would be prosecuted. The editor/owner of the magazine, [Rudolf Augstein](/wiki/Rudolf_Augstein "Rudolf Augstein") spent some time in jail before the public outcry over the breaking of laws on freedom of the press became too loud to be ignored. The FDP members of Adenauer's cabinet resigned from the government, demanding the resignation of [Franz Josef Strauss](/wiki/Franz_Josef_Strauss "Franz Josef Strauss"), Defence Minister, who had decidedly overstepped his competence during the crisis. Adenauer was still wounded by his brief run for president, and this episode damaged his reputation even further. He announced that he would step down in the fall of 1963. His successor was to be Ludwig Erhard.[[15]](#cite_note-19)

In the early 1960s, the rate of economic growth slowed down significantly. In 1962 growth rate was 4.7% and the following year, 2.0%. After a brief recovery, the growth rate slowed again into a recession, with no growth in 1967.

A new coalition was formed to deal with this problem. Erhard stepped down in 1966 and was succeeded by [Kurt Georg Kiesinger](/wiki/Kurt_Georg_Kiesinger "Kurt Georg Kiesinger"). He led a [grand coalition](/wiki/Grand_coalition "Grand coalition") between West Germany's two largest parties, the CDU/CSU and the [Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") (SPD). This was important for the introduction of new [emergency acts](/wiki/German_Emergency_Acts "German Emergency Acts"): the grand coalition gave the ruling parties the two-thirds majority of votes required for their ratification. These controversial acts allowed basic constitutional rights such as [freedom of movement](/wiki/Freedom_of_movement "Freedom of movement") to be limited in case of a state of emergency.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/04/Rudi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rudi.jpg)

[Rudi Dutschke](/wiki/Rudi_Dutschke "Rudi Dutschke"), student leader

During the time leading up to the passing of the laws, there was fierce opposition to them, above all by the [Free Democratic Party](/wiki/Free_Democratic_Party_(Germany) "Free Democratic Party (Germany)"), the rising [West German student movement](/wiki/West_German_student_movement "West German student movement"), a group calling itself *Notstand der Demokratie* ("Democracy in Crisis") and members of the Campaign against Nuclear Armament. A key event in the development of open democratic debate occurred in 1967, when the [Shah of Iran](/wiki/List_of_monarchs_of_Persia "List of monarchs of Persia"), [Mohammad Reza Pahlavi](/wiki/Mohammad_Reza_Pahlavi "Mohammad Reza Pahlavi"), visited West Berlin. Several thousand demonstrators gathered outside the Opera House where he was to attend a special performance. Supporters of the Shah (later known as *Jubelperser*), armed with staves and bricks attacked the protesters while the police stood by and watched. A demonstration in the centre was being forcibly dispersed when a bystander named [Benno Ohnesorg](/wiki/Killing_of_Benno_Ohnesorg "Killing of Benno Ohnesorg") was shot in the head and killed by a plainclothes policeman. (It has now been established that the policeman, Kurras, was a paid spy of the East German security forces.)[[16]](#cite_note-20) Protest demonstrations continued, and calls for more active opposition by some groups of students were made.[*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] In a massive campaign against the protesters, the press, especially the [tabloid](/wiki/Tabloid_journalism "Tabloid journalism") *[Bild](/wiki/Bild "Bild")-Zeitung* newspaper, described these as a massive disruption to life in Berlin. Protests against the [US intervention in Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"), mingled with anger over the vigour with which demonstrations were repressed led to mounting militance among the students at the universities in Berlin. One of the most prominent campaigners was a young man from East Germany called [Rudi Dutschke](/wiki/Rudi_Dutschke "Rudi Dutschke") who also criticised the forms of capitalism that were to be seen in West Berlin. Just before Easter 1968, a young man tried to kill Dutschke as he bicycled to the student union, seriously injuring him. All over West Germany, thousands demonstrated against the Springer newspapers which were seen as the prime cause of the violence against students. Trucks carrying newspapers were set on fire and windows in office buildings broken.[[17]](#cite_note-Kraushaar-21)

In the wake of these demonstrations, in which the question of America's role in Vietnam began to play a bigger role, came a desire among the students to find out more about the role of the parent-generation in the Nazi era. The proceedings of the [War Crimes Tribunal at Nuremberg](/wiki/Nuremberg_trials "Nuremberg trials") had been widely publicised in Germany; the attorney [Fritz Bauer](/wiki/Fritz_Bauer "Fritz Bauer") gathered evidence on the guards of the [*Auschwitz* concentration camp](/wiki/Auschwitz_concentration_camp "Auschwitz concentration camp"), and [about twenty](/wiki/Frankfurt_Auschwitz_trials "Frankfurt Auschwitz trials")[*[specify](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources")*] [were put on trial in Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt_Auschwitz_trials "Frankfurt Auschwitz trials") in 1963. Daily newspaper reports and visits by school classes to the proceedings revealed to the German public the nature of the concentration camp system, and it became evident that [the *Shoah*](/wiki/The_Holocaust "The Holocaust") was of vastly greater dimensions than the German population had believed. The term "Holocaust" for the systematic mass-murder of Jews first came into use in 1979, when a 1978 [American mini-series with that name](/wiki/Holocaust_(miniseries) "Holocaust (miniseries)") was shown on West German television. The processes set in motion by the Auschwitz trial reverberated decades later.

The calling in question of the actions and policies of government led to a new climate of debate. The issues of emancipation, colonialism, environmentalism and grassroots democracy were discussed at all levels of society. In 1979, the environmental party, the Greens, reached the 5% limit required to obtain parliamentary seats in the [Free Hanseatic City of Bremen](/wiki/Bremen_(state) "Bremen (state)") provincial election. Also of great significance was the steady growth of a [feminist movement](/wiki/Feminism_in_Germany "Feminism in Germany") in which women demonstrated for equal rights. Until 1977, a married woman had to have the permission of her husband if she wanted to take on a job or open a bank account.[[18]](#cite_note-22) Further reforms in 1979 to parental rights law gave equal legal rights to the mother and the father, abolishing the legal authority of the father.[[19]](#cite_note-23) Parallel to this, a gay movement began to grow in the larger cities, especially in West Berlin, where homosexuality had been widely accepted during the twenties in the Weimar Republic.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/RAF-Logo.svg/250px-RAF-Logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:RAF-Logo.svg)

Logo of the [Red Army Faction](/wiki/Red_Army_Faction "Red Army Faction")

Anger over the treatment of demonstrators following the death of Benno Ohnesorg and the attack on Rudi Dutschke, coupled with growing frustration over the lack of success in achieving their aims led to growing militance among students and their supporters. In May 1968, three young people set fire to two department stores in Frankfurt; they were brought to trial and made clear to the court that they regarded their action as a legitimate act in what they described as the "struggle against imperialism".[[17]](#cite_note-Kraushaar-21) The student movement began to split into different factions, ranging from the unattached liberals to the [Maoists](/wiki/Maoism "Maoism") and supporters of direct action in every form‚ÄĒthe anarchists. Several groups set as their objective the aim of radicalising the industrial workers; taking an example from activities in Italy of the [Red Brigades](/wiki/Red_Brigades "Red Brigades") (*Brigate Rosse*), many students went to work in the factories, but with little or no success[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]. The most notorious of the underground groups was the [Red Army Faction](/wiki/Red_Army_Faction "Red Army Faction"), which began by making bank raids to finance their activities and eventually went underground having killed a number of policemen, several bystanders and eventually two prominent West Germans, whom they had taken captive in order to force the release of prisoners sympathetic to their ideas. In the 1990s, attacks were still being committed under the name "RAF". The last action took place in 1993 and the group announced it was giving up its activities in 1998. Evidence that the groups had been infiltrated by German Intelligence undercover agents has since[*[when?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] emerged, partly through the insistence of the son of the State Counsel [Siegfried Buback](/wiki/Siegfried_Buback "Siegfried Buback"), who had been shot by members of the RAF in 1977.[[20]](#cite_note-24)

### Willy Brandt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Germany&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Willy Brandt")]

Main article: [Willy Brandt](/wiki/Willy_Brandt "Willy Brandt")

In October 1969, Willy Brandt became chancellor. He maintained West Germany's close alignment with the United States and focused on strengthening [European integration](/wiki/European_integration "European integration") in western Europe, while launching the new policy of *[Ostpolitik](/wiki/Ostpolitik "Ostpolitik")* aimed at improving relations with Eastern Europe. Brandt was controversial on both the right wing, for his *Ostpolitik*, and on the left wing, for his support of [right-wing authoritarian](/wiki/Right-wing_dictatorship "Right-wing dictatorship") regimes and of American policies, including the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War"). The [Brandt Report](/wiki/Brandt_Report "Brandt Report") became a recognised measure for describing the general [North-South divide](/wiki/Global_North_and_Global_South "Global North and Global South") in world economics and politics between an affluent North and a poor South. Brandt was also known for his fierce [anti-communist](/wiki/Anti-communism "Anti-communism") policies at the domestic level, culminating in the *[Radikalenerlass](/wiki/Berufsverbot "Berufsverbot")* (Anti-Radical Decree) in 1972. In 1970, while visiting a memorial to the [Warsaw Ghetto Uprising](/wiki/Warsaw_Ghetto_Uprising "Warsaw Ghetto Uprising") crushed by the Germans, Brandt unexpectedly knelt and meditated in silence, a moment remembered as the [Kniefall von Warschau](/wiki/Kniefall_von_Warschau "Kniefall von Warschau").

Brandt resigned as chancellor in 1974, after [G√ľnter Guillaume](/wiki/G%C3%BCnter_Guillaume "G√ľnter Guillaume"), one of his closest aides, [was exposed](/wiki/Guillaume_affair "Guillaume affair") as an agent of the [Stasi](/wiki/Stasi "Stasi"), the [East German](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany") [secret service](/wiki/Secret_service "Secret service").

### Helmut Schmidt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Germany&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Helmut Schmidt")]

Finance Minister [Helmut Schmidt](/wiki/Helmut_Schmidt "Helmut Schmidt") (SPD) formed a coalition and he served as Chancellor from 1974 to 1982. [Hans-Dietrich Genscher](/wiki/Hans-Dietrich_Genscher "Hans-Dietrich Genscher"), a leading FDP official, became Vice-Chancellor and Foreign Minister. Schmidt, a strong supporter of the European Community (EC) and the Atlantic alliance, emphasised his commitment to "the political unification of Europe in partnership with the USA".[[21]](#cite_note-25) Mounting external problems forced Schmidt to concentrate on foreign policy and limited the domestic reforms that he could carry out. The USSR upgraded its intermediate-range missiles, which Schmidt complained was an unacceptable threat to the balance of nuclear power, because it increased the likelihood of political coercion and required a western response. NATO responded in the form of its [twin-track policy](/wiki/NATO_Double-Track_Decision "NATO Double-Track Decision"). The domestic reverberations were serious inside the SPD, and undermined its coalition with the FDP.[[22]](#cite_note-26) One of his major successes, in collaboration with French President [Val√©ry Giscard d'Estaing](/wiki/Val%C3%A9ry_Giscard_d%27Estaing "Val√©ry Giscard d'Estaing"), was the launching of the [European Monetary System](/wiki/European_Monetary_System "European Monetary System") (EMS) in April 1978.[[23]](#cite_note-27)

### Helmut Kohl

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Germany&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Helmut Kohl")]

In October 1982 the SPD‚ÄďFDP coalition fell apart when the FDP joined forces with the CDU/CSU to elect CDU Chairman [Helmut Kohl](/wiki/Helmut_Kohl "Helmut Kohl") as Chancellor in a [constructive vote of no confidence](/wiki/Constructive_vote_of_no_confidence "Constructive vote of no confidence"). Following national elections in March 1983, Kohl emerged in firm control of both the government and the CDU. The CDU/CSU fell just short of an absolute majority, due to the entry into the Bundestag of the [Greens](/wiki/Alliance_90/The_Greens "Alliance 90/The Greens"), who received 5.6% of the vote.

In January 1987 the Kohl‚ÄďGenscher government was returned to office, but the FDP and the Greens gained at the expense of the larger parties. Kohl's CDU and its Bavarian sister party, the CSU, slipped from 48.8% of the vote in 1983 to 44.3%. The SPD fell to 37%; long-time SPD Chairman Brandt subsequently resigned in April 1987 and was succeeded by [Hans-Jochen Vogel](/wiki/Hans-Jochen_Vogel "Hans-Jochen Vogel"). The FDP's share rose from 7% to 9.1%, its best showing since 1980. The Greens' share rose to 8.3% from their 1983 share of 5.6%.

### Reunification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Germany&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Reunification")]

Main article: [German reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification")

See also: [Old states of Germany](/wiki/Old_states_of_Germany "Old states of Germany")

With the [collapse of eastern bloc](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1989 "Revolutions of 1989") in 1989, symbolised by the opening of the [Berlin Wall](/wiki/Berlin_Wall "Berlin Wall"), there was a rapid move towards [German reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification"); and [a final settlement of the post-war special status of Germany](/wiki/Treaty_on_the_Final_Settlement_with_Respect_to_Germany "Treaty on the Final Settlement with Respect to Germany"). Following democratic elections, East Germany declared its accession to the Federal Republic subject to the terms of the Unification Treaty between the two states; and then both West Germany and East Germany radically amended their respective constitutions in accordance with that Treaty's provisions. East Germany then dissolved itself, and its five post-war states (*L√§nder*) were reconstituted, along with the reunited Berlin which ended its special status and formed an additional *Land*. They formally joined the Federal Republic on 3 October 1990, raising the number of states from 10 to 16, ending the division of Germany. The expanded Federal Republic retained West Germany's political culture and continued its existing memberships in international organisations, as well as its Western foreign policy alignment and affiliation to Western alliances like NATO and the European Union.

The official German reunification ceremony on 3 October 1990 was held at the [*Reichstag* building](/wiki/Reichstag_building "Reichstag building"), including [Chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor_of_Germany#Federal_Chancellor_of_the_Federal_Republic_of_Germany_(1949‚Äďpresent) "Chancellor of Germany") [Helmut Kohl](/wiki/Helmut_Kohl "Helmut Kohl"), President [Richard von Weizs√§cker](/wiki/Richard_von_Weizs%C3%A4cker "Richard von Weizs√§cker"), former Chancellor [Willy Brandt](/wiki/Willy_Brandt "Willy Brandt") and many others. One day later, the parliament of the united Germany would assemble in an act of symbolism in the Reichstag building.

However, at that time, the role of Berlin had not yet been decided upon. Only after a fierce debate, considered by many as one of the most memorable sessions of parliament, the *[Bundestag](/wiki/Bundestag "Bundestag")* concluded on 20 June 1991, with quite a slim majority, that both government and parliament should move to [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") from [Bonn](/wiki/Bonn "Bonn").

### Government structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Germany&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Government structure")]

West Germany was a federal parliamentary republic with the national capital being in [Bonn](/wiki/Bonn "Bonn"). The executive branch consisted of a President and a Chancellor with the President being the head of state and a largely ceremonial role while the Chancellor was the head of the government and functioned in a capacity similar to that of a prime minister. The Chancellor was elected by the legislature for a four-year term and could not be removed from office during that term except for if the Bundestag had selected a replacement.[[24]](#cite_note-:0-28)

The country had a bicameral legislature consisting of an upper house, the [Bundesrat](/wiki/German_Bundesrat "German Bundesrat") and a lower house, the [Bundestag](/wiki/Bundestag "Bundestag").[[24]](#cite_note-:0-28)

The Bundesrat had 45 members and they were elected by the Laender (states).[[24]](#cite_note-:0-28) The amount of voting members in the Bundestag varied over time with initially 402 in 1949 to 497 in 1987, with peak membership being at 499 in 1961.[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/West_Germany#endnote_There_was_a_delegation_from_West_Berlin_but_they_were_non-voting_members_and_aren't_included_in_the_source.)[[25]](#cite_note-29) Members were elected from [West Berlin](/wiki/West_Berlin "West Berlin") to the Bundesrat and the Bundestag but they could not vote.[[26]](#cite_note-30)[[27]](#cite_note-31)

### Political dynamics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Germany&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Political dynamics")]

Political life in West Germany was remarkably stable and orderly. The [Adenauer](/wiki/Konrad_Adenauer "Konrad Adenauer") era (1949‚Äď63) was followed by a brief period under [Ludwig Erhard](/wiki/Ludwig_Erhard "Ludwig Erhard") (1963‚Äď66) who, in turn, was replaced by [Kurt Georg Kiesinger](/wiki/Kurt_Georg_Kiesinger "Kurt Georg Kiesinger") (1966‚Äď69). All governments between 1949 and 1966 were formed by the united caucus of the [Christian-Democratic Union](/wiki/Christian_Democratic_Union_(Germany) "Christian Democratic Union (Germany)") (CDU) and [Christian Social Union](/wiki/Christian_Social_Union_of_Bavaria "Christian Social Union of Bavaria") (CSU), either alone or in coalition with the smaller [Free Democratic Party](/wiki/Free_Democratic_Party_(Germany) "Free Democratic Party (Germany)") (FDP) or other right leaning parties.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F038505-0006%2C_Bonn%2C_Amtsantritt_Kabinett_Brandt_bei_Heinemann.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F038505-0006%2C_Bonn%2C_Amtsantritt_Kabinett_Brandt_bei_Heinemann.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F038505-0006,_Bonn,_Amtsantritt_Kabinett_Brandt_bei_Heinemann.jpg)

The Brandt cabinet of 1969 on the steps of President Heinemanns's residence in Bonn, the [Villa Hammerschmidt](/wiki/Villa_Hammerschmidt "Villa Hammerschmidt")

Kiesinger's 1966‚Äď69 "Grand Coalition" was between West Germany's two largest parties, the CDU/CSU and the [Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") (SPD). This was important for the introduction of new emergency acts‚ÄĒthe Grand Coalition gave the ruling parties the two-thirds majority of votes required to see them in. These controversial acts allowed basic constitutional rights such as freedom of movement to be limited in case of a [state of emergency](/wiki/State_of_emergency "State of emergency").

Leading up to the passing of the laws, there was fierce opposition to them, above all by the FDP, the rising [German student movement](/wiki/German_student_movement "German student movement"), a group calling itself [Notstand der Demokratie](/w/index.php?title=Notstand_der_Demokratie&action=edit&redlink=1 "Notstand der Demokratie (page does not exist)")¬†[[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kuratorium_%E2%80%9ENotstand_der_Demokratie%E2%80%9C "de:Kuratorium ‚ÄěNotstand der Demokratie‚Äú")] ("Democracy in a State of Emergency") and the [labour unions](/wiki/Trade_union "Trade union"). Demonstrations and protests grew in number, and in 1967 the student [Benno Ohnesorg](/wiki/Benno_Ohnesorg "Benno Ohnesorg") was shot in the head by a policeman. The press, especially the [tabloid](/wiki/Tabloid_(newspaper_format) "Tabloid (newspaper format)") *[Bild-Zeitung](/wiki/Bild-Zeitung "Bild-Zeitung")* newspaper, launched a campaign against the protesters.

By 1968, a stronger desire to confront the [Nazi](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism") past had come into being. In the 1970s environmentalism and [anti-nationalism](/wiki/Anti-nationalism "Anti-nationalism") became fundamental values among left-wing Germans. As a result, in 1979 [the Greens](/wiki/Alliance_%2790/The_Greens "Alliance '90/The Greens") were able to reach the 5% minimum required to obtain parliamentary seats in the [Free Hanseatic City of Bremen](/wiki/Bremen_(state) "Bremen (state)") state election, and with the foundation of the national party in 1980 developed into one of the most politically successful green movements in the world.

Another result of the unrest in the 1960s was the founding of the [Red Army Faction](/wiki/Red_Army_Faction "Red Army Faction") (RAF). The RAF was active from 1968, carrying out a succession of terrorist attacks in West Germany during the 1970s. Even in the 1990s, attacks were still being committed under the name *RAF*. The last action took place in 1993, and in 1998 the group announced it was ceasing activities.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F074398-0021_Kohl_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F074398-0021_Kohl_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F074398-0021_Kohl_(cropped).jpg)

[Helmut Kohl](/wiki/Helmut_Kohl "Helmut Kohl") in 1987

In the 1969 election, the SPD gained enough votes to form a coalition government with the FDP. SPD leader and Chancellor [Willy Brandt](/wiki/Willy_Brandt "Willy Brandt") remained head of government until May 1974, when he resigned after the [Guillaume affair](/wiki/Guillaume_affair "Guillaume affair"), in which a senior member of his staff was uncovered as a spy for the East German intelligence service, the *[Stasi](/wiki/Stasi "Stasi")*. However, the affair is widely considered to have been merely a trigger for Brandt's resignation, not a fundamental cause. Instead, Brandt, dogged by scandal relating to alcohol and depression[[28]](#cite_note-32)[[29]](#cite_note-33) as well as the economic fallout of the [1973 oil crisis](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis"), almost seems simply to have had enough. As Brandt himself later said, "I was exhausted, for reasons which had nothing to do with the process going on at the time".[[30]](#cite_note-34)

Finance Minister Helmut Schmidt (SPD) then formed a government, continuing the SPD‚ÄďFDP coalition. He served as Chancellor from 1974 to 1982. Hans-Dietrich Genscher, a leading FDP official, was Vice-Chancellor and Foreign Minister in the same years. Schmidt, a strong supporter of the [European Community](/wiki/European_Community "European Community") (EC) and the Atlantic alliance, emphasised his commitment to "the political unification of Europe in partnership with the USA".

The goals of SPD and FDP drifted apart in the late 1970s and early 1980s. On 1 October 1982 the FDP joined forces with the CDU/CSU to elect CDU Chairman [Helmut Kohl](/wiki/Helmut_Kohl "Helmut Kohl") as Chancellor in a [constructive vote of no confidence](/wiki/Constructive_vote_of_no_confidence "Constructive vote of no confidence"). Following national elections in March 1983, Kohl emerged in firm control of both the government and the CDU. The CDU/CSU fell just short of an absolute majority due to the entry into the Bundestag of the Greens, who received 5.6% of the vote.

In January 1987 the Kohl‚ÄďGenscher government was returned to office, but the FDP and the Greens gained at the expense of the larger parties. The Social Democrats concluded that not only were the Greens unlikely to form a coalition, but also that such a coalition would be far from a majority. Neither condition changed until 1998.

### Denazification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Germany&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Denazification")]

See also: [Denazification](/wiki/Denazification "Denazification"), [Naumann Circle](/wiki/Naumann_Circle "Naumann Circle"), and [List of companies involved in the Holocaust](/wiki/List_of_companies_involved_in_the_Holocaust "List of companies involved in the Holocaust")

Denazification was an [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") initiative to rid German politics, judiciary, society, culture, press and economy of [Nazi](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism") ideology and personnel following the [Second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"). It was carried out by removing those who had been [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party") or [SS](/wiki/Schutzstaffel "Schutzstaffel") members from positions of power and influence, by disbanding the organisations associated with Nazism, and by trying prominent Nazis for [war crimes](/wiki/War_crime "War crime").[[31]](#cite_note-taylor11-35) The program was hugely unpopular in West Germany and was opposed by the new government of Konrad Adenauer.[[32]](#cite_note-36) In 1951, several laws were passed granting amnesties and ending denazification. As a result, many people with a former Nazi past ended up again in the political apparatus of West Germany.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEZentnerBed√ľrftig1997189‚Äď190-37)

Between 1951 and 1953, there was even an effort by a clandestine group of former Nazi functionaries, known as the [Naumann Circle](/wiki/Naumann_Circle "Naumann Circle"), to infiltrate the Free Democratic Party (FDP) in order to lay the groundwork for an eventual return to power. Although this effort was exposed and disrupted, many former Nazis still attained positions of power and influence in the political system.[[34]](#cite_note-38) West German President (1974‚Äď1979) [Walter Scheel](/wiki/Walter_Scheel "Walter Scheel") and Chancellor (1966‚Äď1969) [Kurt Georg Kiesinger](/wiki/Kurt_Georg_Kiesinger "Kurt Georg Kiesinger") were both former members of the Nazi Party. Konrad Adenauer's [State Secretary](/wiki/State_Secretary "State Secretary") [Hans Globke](/wiki/Hans_Globke "Hans Globke") had played a major role in drafting the antisemitic [Nuremberg Race Laws](/wiki/Nuremberg_Laws "Nuremberg Laws") in Nazi Germany.[[35]](#cite_note-39) In 1957, 77% of the [West German Ministry of Justice](/wiki/Federal_Ministry_of_Justice_and_Consumer_Protection "Federal Ministry of Justice and Consumer Protection")'s senior officials were former Nazi Party members.[[36]](#cite_note-40)

### Geographical distribution of government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Germany&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Geographical distribution of government")]

In West Germany, most of the political agencies and buildings were located in Bonn, while the [German Stock Market](/wiki/DAX "DAX") was located in [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt") which became the economic center. The judicial branch of both the German [Federal Constitutional Court](/wiki/Federal_Constitutional_Court "Federal Constitutional Court") (*Bundesverfassungsgericht*) and the highest Court of Appeals, were located in [Karlsruhe](/wiki/Karlsruhe "Karlsruhe").

The West German government was known to be much more [decentralised](/wiki/Decentralised "Decentralised") than its [state socialist](/wiki/State_socialism "State socialism") East German counterpart, the former being a [federal state](/wiki/Federal_state "Federal state") and the latter a [unitary one](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state"). Whilst East Germany was divided into 15 administrative districts (*Bezirke*), which were merely local branches of the national government, West Germany was divided into states (*L√§nder*) with independently elected state parliaments and control of the *[Bundesrat](/wiki/Bundesrat_of_Germany "Bundesrat of Germany")*, the second legislative chamber of the Federal Government.

### Foreign relations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Germany&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Foreign relations")]

#### Position towards East Germany

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Germany&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Position towards East Germany")]

See also: [German reunification](/wiki/German_reunification "German reunification")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F031406-0017%2C_Erfurt%2C_Treffen_Willy_Brandt_mit_Willi_Stoph.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F031406-0017%2C_Erfurt%2C_Treffen_Willy_Brandt_mit_Willi_Stoph.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F031406-0017,_Erfurt,_Treffen_Willy_Brandt_mit_Willi_Stoph.jpg)

[Willy Brandt](/wiki/Willy_Brandt "Willy Brandt") and [Willi Stoph](/wiki/Willi_Stoph "Willi Stoph") in [Erfurt](/wiki/Erfurt "Erfurt"), 1970; the first time a Chancellor met a GDR prime minister

The official position of West Germany concerning East Germany at the outset was that the West German government was the only democratically elected, and therefore the only legitimate, representative of the German people. According to the [Hallstein Doctrine](/wiki/Hallstein_Doctrine "Hallstein Doctrine"), any country (with the exception of the USSR) that recognised the authorities of the [German Democratic Republic](/wiki/German_Democratic_Republic "German Democratic Republic") would not have diplomatic relations with West Germany.

In the early 1970s, Willy Brandt's policy of "*Neue [Ostpolitik](/wiki/Ostpolitik "Ostpolitik")*" led to a form of mutual recognition between East and West Germany. The [Treaty of Moscow](/wiki/Treaty_of_Moscow_(1970) "Treaty of Moscow (1970)") (August 1970), the [Treaty of Warsaw](/wiki/Treaty_of_Warsaw_(1970) "Treaty of Warsaw (1970)") (December 1970), the [Four Power Agreement on Berlin](/wiki/Four_Power_Agreement_on_Berlin "Four Power Agreement on Berlin") (September 1971), the [Transit Agreement](/wiki/Transit_Agreement_(1972) "Transit Agreement (1972)") (May 1972), and the [Basic Treaty](/wiki/Basic_Treaty,_1972 "Basic Treaty, 1972") (December 1972) helped to normalise relations between East and West Germany and led to both German states joining the United Nations. The Hallstein Doctrine was relinquished, and West Germany ceased to claim an [exclusive mandate](/wiki/Exclusive_mandate "Exclusive mandate") for Germany as a whole.

Following the Ostpolitik, the West German view was that East Germany was a *de facto* government within a single German nation and a *de jure* state organisation of parts of Germany outside the Federal Republic. The Federal Republic continued to maintain that it could not within its own structures recognise the GDR *de jure* as a sovereign state under international law; while at the same time acknowledging that, within the structures of international law, the GDR was an independent sovereign state. By distinction, West Germany then viewed itself as being within its own boundaries, not only the *de facto* and *de jure* government, but also the sole *de jure* legitimate representative of a dormant "Germany as whole".[[37]](#cite_note-41) The two Germanies relinquished any claim to represent the other internationally, which they acknowledged as necessarily implying a mutual recognition of each other as both capable of representing their own populations *de jure* in participating in international bodies and agreements, such as the United Nations and the [Helsinki Final Act](/wiki/Helsinki_Accords "Helsinki Accords").

This assessment of the Basic Treaty was confirmed in a decision of the [Federal Constitutional Court](/wiki/Federal_Constitutional_Court "Federal Constitutional Court") in 1973;[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKommers2012308-42)

:   "... the German Democratic Republic is in the international law sense a State and as such a subject of international law. This finding is independent of recognition in international law of the German Democratic Republic by the Federal Republic of Germany. Such recognition has not only never been formally pronounced by the Federal Republic of Germany but on the contrary repeatedly explicitly rejected. If the conduct of the Federal Republic of Germany towards the German Democratic Republic is assessed in the light of its d√©tente policy, in particular the conclusion of the Treaty as de facto recognition, then it can only be understood as de facto recognition of a special kind. The special feature of this Treaty is that while it is a bilateral Treaty between two States, to which the rules of international law apply and which like any other international treaty possesses validity, it is between two States that are parts of a still existing, albeit incapable of action as not being reorganized, comprehensive State of the Whole of Germany with a single body politic."[[39]](#cite_note-43)

The West German Constitution (*Grundgesetz*, "Basic Law") provided two articles for the unification with other parts of Germany:

* Article 23 provided the possibility for other parts of Germany to join the Federal Republic (under the constitution of the Federal Republic of Germany).
* Article 146 provided the possibility for unification of all parts of Germany under a new constitution.

After the peaceful revolution of 1989 in East Germany, the Volkskammer of the GDR on 23 August 1990 declared the accession of East Germany to the Federal Republic under Article 23 of the Basic Law and thus initiated the process of reunification, to come into effect on 3 October 1990. Nevertheless, the act of reunification itself (with its many specific terms and conditions; including fundamental amendments to the West German Basic Law) was achieved constitutionally by the subsequent Unification Treaty of 31 August 1990; that is through a binding agreement between the former GDR and the Federal Republic now recognising each another as separate sovereign states in international law.[[40]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKommers2012309-44) This treaty was then voted into effect on 20 September 1990 by both the Volkskammer and the [Bundestag](/wiki/Bundestag "Bundestag") by the constitutionally required two-thirds majorities; effecting on the one hand, the extinction of the GDR and the re-establishment of *[L√§nder](/wiki/New_states_of_Germany "New states of Germany")* on the territory of East Germany; and on the other, the agreed amendments to the Basic Law of the Federal Republic. Amongst these amendments was the repeal of the very Article 23 in respect of which the GDR had nominally declared its postdated accession to the Federal Republic.

The two German states entered into a [currency](/wiki/Monetary_union "Monetary union") and [customs union](/wiki/Customs_union "Customs union") in July 1990, and on 3 October 1990, the [German Democratic Republic](/wiki/German_Democratic_Republic "German Democratic Republic") dissolved and the re-established [five East German *L√§nder*](/wiki/New_states_of_Germany "New states of Germany") (as well as a unified Berlin) joined the Federal Republic of Germany, bringing an end to the East‚ÄďWest divide.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Germany&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Economy")]

### Economic miracle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Germany&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Economic miracle")]

The West German *[Wirtschaftswunder](/wiki/Wirtschaftswunder "Wirtschaftswunder")* ("economic miracle", coined by *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")*) began in 1950. This improvement was sustained by the currency reform of 1948 which replaced the *[Reichsmark](/wiki/Reichsmark "Reichsmark")* with the *[Deutsche Mark](/wiki/Deutsche_Mark "Deutsche Mark")* and halted rampant inflation. The Allied dismantling of the West German coal and steel industry finally ended in 1950.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F038788-0006%2C_Wolfsburg%2C_VW_Autowerk%2C_K%C3%A4fer.jpg/250px-Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F038788-0006%2C_Wolfsburg%2C_VW_Autowerk%2C_K%C3%A4fer.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bundesarchiv_B_145_Bild-F038788-0006,_Wolfsburg,_VW_Autowerk,_K%C3%A4fer.jpg)

The [Volkswagen Beetle](/wiki/Volkswagen_Beetle "Volkswagen Beetle")¬†‚Äď for many years the most successful car in the world¬†‚Äď on the assembly line in Wolfsburg factory, 1973

As demand for consumer goods increased after World War II, the resulting shortage helped overcome lingering resistance to the purchase of German products. At the time, Germany had a large pool of skilled and cheap labour, partly as a result of the flight and expulsion of Germans from Central and Eastern Europe, which affected up to 16.5 million Germans. This helped Germany to more than double the value of its exports during the war. Apart from these factors, hard work and long hours at full capacity among the population and in the late 1950s and 1960s extra labour supplied by thousands of *[Gastarbeiter](/wiki/Gastarbeiter "Gastarbeiter")* ("guest workers") provided a vital base for the economic upturn. This would have implications later on for successive German governments as they tried to assimilate this group of workers.[[41]](#cite_note-45)

With the dropping of Allied reparations, the freeing of German intellectual property and the impact of the [Marshall Plan](/wiki/Marshall_Plan "Marshall Plan") stimulus, West Germany developed one of the strongest economies in the world, almost as strong as before the Second World War. The [East German economy](/wiki/Economy_of_East_Germany "Economy of East Germany") showed a certain growth, but not as much as in West Germany, partly because of continued reparations to the USSR.[[42]](#cite_note-46)

In 1952, West Germany became part of the [European Coal and Steel Community](/wiki/European_Coal_and_Steel_Community "European Coal and Steel Community"), which would later evolve into the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"). On 5 May 1955 West Germany was declared to have the "authority of a sovereign state".[[d]](#cite_note-Sovereignty-15) The [British](/wiki/British_Armed_Forces "British Armed Forces"), [French](/wiki/Military_of_France "Military of France") and [US militaries](/wiki/Military_of_the_United_States "Military of the United States") remained in the country, just as the [Soviet Army](/wiki/Red_Army "Red Army") remained in East Germany. Four days after obtaining the "authority of a sovereign state" in 1955, West Germany joined NATO. The UK and the USA retained an especially strong presence in West Germany, acting as a deterrent in case of a Soviet invasion. In 1976, West Germany became one of the founding nations of the [Group of Six](/wiki/Group_of_Six "Group of Six") (G6). In 1973, West Germany‚ÄĒhome to roughly 1.26% of the world's population‚ÄĒfeatured the world's [fourth-highest GDP](/wiki/List_of_regions_by_past_GDP_(PPP) "List of regions by past GDP (PPP)") of 814,796 million compared to [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany")'s 129,969 million,[[43]](#cite_note-AM-47) together combining for 944,755 million and accounting for 5.9% of the world total.[[44]](#cite_note-AM2-48) In 1987, the FRG held a 7.4% share of total world production.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Germany&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Culture")]

See also: [Culture of Germany](/wiki/Culture_of_Germany "Culture of Germany")

In many aspects, German culture continued in spite of the dictatorship and wartime. Old and new forms coexisted next to each other, and the American influence, already strong in the 1920s, grew.[[50]](#cite_note-54)

### Literary scene

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Germany&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Literary scene")]

Besides the interest in the older generation of writers, new authors emerged on the background of the experiences of war and after war period. [Wolfgang Borchert](/wiki/Wolfgang_Borchert "Wolfgang Borchert"), a former soldier who died young in 1947, is one of the best known representatives of the *[Tr√ľmmerliteratur](/wiki/Tr%C3%BCmmerliteratur "Tr√ľmmerliteratur")*. [Heinrich B√∂ll](/wiki/Heinrich_B%C3%B6ll "Heinrich B√∂ll") is considered an observer of the young Federal Republic from the 1950s to the 1970s, and caused some political controversies because of his increasingly critical view on society.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The [Frankfurt Book Fair](/wiki/Frankfurt_Book_Fair "Frankfurt Book Fair") (and its [Peace Prize of the German Book Trade](/wiki/Peace_Prize_of_the_German_Book_Trade "Peace Prize of the German Book Trade")) soon developed into a regarded institution. Exemplary for West Germany's literature are‚ÄĒamong others‚ÄĒ[Siegfried Lenz](/wiki/Siegfried_Lenz "Siegfried Lenz") (with *[The German Lesson](/wiki/The_German_Lesson "The German Lesson")*) and [G√ľnter Grass](/wiki/G%C3%BCnter_Grass "G√ľnter Grass") (with *[The Tin Drum](/wiki/The_Tin_Drum "The Tin Drum")* and *[The Flounder](/wiki/The_Flounder "The Flounder")*).

### Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=West_Germany&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Sport")]

In the 20th century, association football became the largest sport in Germany. The [Germany national football team](/wiki/Germany_national_football_team "Germany national football team"), established in 1900, continued its tradition based in the Federal Republic of Germany, winning the [1954 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1954_FIFA_World_Cup "1954 FIFA World Cup") in a stunning upset dubbed the [miracle of Bern](/wiki/1954_FIFA_World_Cup_Final "1954 FIFA World Cup Final"). Earlier, the German team was not considered part of the international top. The [1974 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1974_FIFA_World_Cup "1974 FIFA World Cup") was held in West German cities and West Berlin. After having been beaten by their East German counterparts in the first round, the team of the [German Football Association](/wiki/German_Football_Association "German Football Association") won the cup again, defeating the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") 2‚Äď1 in the final. With the process of unification in full swing in the summer of 1990, the Germans won a third World Cup, with players that had been capped for East Germany not yet permitted to contribute. European championships have been won too, in 1972 and 1980.[[51]](#cite_note-55)[[52]](#cite_note-56)

After both [Olympic Games of 1936](/wiki/1936_Summer_Olympics "1936 Summer Olympics") had been held in Germany, [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich") was selected to host the [1972 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1972_Summer_Olympics "1972 Summer Olympics"). These were also the first summer games in which the East Germans showed up with the separate flag and anthem of the GDR. Since the 1950s, [Germany at the Olympics](/wiki/Germany_at_the_Olympics "Germany at the Olympics") had been represented by a united team led by the pre-war German NOC officials as the IOC had denied East German demands for a separate team. At the [1956 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1956_Summer_Olympics "1956 Summer Olympics"), the Olympic teams of West Germany, East Germany, and Saarland were merged to represent Germany together. Four years earlier Saarland had attended as separate teams while East Germany had not attended. After 1956, 1962, and 1964; East Germany competed in the Summer Olympics as a separate member of the IOC.

The 800-page *Doping in Germany from 1950 to today* study details how the West German government helped fund a wide-scale [doping](/wiki/Doping_in_sport "Doping in sport") programme.[[53]](#cite_note-57)[[54]](#cite_note-58) West Germany encouraged and covered up a culture of doping across many sports for decades.[[55]](#cite_note-59)[[56]](#cite_note-60)

As in 1957, when the Saarland acceded, East German sport organisations ceased to exist in late 1990 as their subdivisions and their members joined their Western counterparts. Thus, the present German organisations and teams in football, Olympics and elsewhere are identical to those that had been informally called "West German" before 1991. The only differences were a larger membership and a different name used by some foreigners. These organisations and teams in turn mostly continued the traditions of those that represented Germany before the Second World War, and even the First World War, thus providing a century-old continuity despite political changes. On the other hand, the separate East German teams and organisations were founded in the 1950s; they were an episode lasting less than four decades, yet quite successful in that time.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

West Germany played 43 matches at the European Championships, more than any other national team.[[57]](#cite_note-61)
